article.body_preview: DHAKA - While the peak electricity supply reached 15,604MW in Bangladesh at the beginning of summer, many areas, mostly outside Dhaka, have been facing long hours of load-shedding and disruption of economic activities. The reason behind the current crisis is not the lower installed capacity, but the inability to buy imported energy to run the existing power plants and interrupted services due to technical inefficiency. llm_connections.evidence_quote: be able to pay for the imported energy to fulfil its demand amid the US dollar crisis
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article explicitly links a country-level 'US dollar crisis' to Bangladesh's ability to pay for imported energy, which matches the feature's scope of exchange-rate (USD/BDT) effects on import costs.

verification.quote_best_match: be able to pay for the imported energy to fulfil its demand amid the US dollar crisis
verification.judge_reasoning: The article contains the sentence stating Bangladesh may not "be able to pay for the imported energy to fulfil its demand amid the US dollar crisis...", which explicitly links the US dollar crisis to a reduced ability to pay for imported energy at the country level.
